pages.text: of pores (larger than 0.01 lm) in C-100-PVPC-S and D-100-PVPC mixtures ( Fig. 7 ). Although porosity, average pore diameter and volume of pores decrease with the use of coarse pumice aggregate, the decrease is not signiﬁcant compared to those obtained with the use of PVPC and ﬁne pumice aggregate. This study conﬁrms the reﬁnement of pore structures of VPCs in presence of pumice as pozzolans (in PVPC) as well as aggregates (both ﬁne and coarse) which can increase impermeability and chloride resistance. 3.3.2. DSC test results The quantity of Ca(OH) 2 in concrete mixes with PVPC and ﬁne pumice aggregate was lower compared to other mixes ( Table 8 ). Lowering of Ca(OH) 2 indicated that the pozzolanic reactivity of ﬁ- nely ground pumice in PVPC and ﬁne pumice aggregate consumed Ca(OH)2 resulting from the hydration of cement. The pozzolanic reaction of pumice with Ca(OH) 2 should be associated with the de- crease of pore size with reﬁnement of pore structure (as conﬁrmed from this study) and to a smaller effective diffusivity for water/ chloride improving the long-term corrosion resistance. 3.3.3. Microhardness and Interfacial transition zone (ITZ) Microhardness testing is a powerful tool for characterizing the micro-structural properties of ITZ of concrete [28]. This testing was applied to characterize the inﬂuence of PVPC and ﬁne/coarse pumice aggregate on the ITZ of VPCs around the aggregates. Fig. 8 shows variation of ITZ ranges of selected VPC mixtures. In- creased microhardness values in the vicinity of the aggregate were observed in all specimens, and this may be due to the presence of stiff inclusions in the excited range around the indentation, which restrains the ﬂow of material under the indentation [24]. Microhardness test showed lower Vickers Hardness Numbers (Hv) in ITZ than those in the middle of the hardened bulk cement pastes. The extent of the weak zone (ITZ range) is about 45 lm for NC (0% coarse VPA- Mix A-0-PC-S). The ITZ range is reduced for VPC mixtures (ranges between 30 and 40 lm) and the Vickers Hardness Numbers (Hv) are also higher in the ITZ compared to NC. The reduction of ITZ range (width) and the higher values of microhard- ness (within the ITZ) conﬁrmed the superior (stronger) ITZ in VPC compared to NC. VPCs with both ﬁne and coarse pumice aggregate (Mixes B-100-PC and D-100-PVPC) exhibited better ITZ compared to those with only coarse pumice aggregate (A-100-PC-S and C- 100-PVPC-S). Concretes with PVPC also showed better ITZ com- pared to those with Portland cement. 3.4. Durability properties of lightweight pumice concrete The durability characteristics in terms of drying shrinkage and water permeability of concrete mixtures are summarized in Tables 6 and 7 . 3.4.1. Drying shrinkage Aggregates with high absorption properties are associated with high shrinkage in concrete and this is conﬁrmed from the increase of drying shrinkage with the increase of coarse pumice aggregate in Series I ( Fig. 9 ). The maximum increase in shrinkage in VPCs with 100% pumice aggregate in Series I ranged between 17% and 34% (with maximum in Type B) compared with NC (Mix A-0-C-S) (Fig. 3 ). The 12-week drying shrinkage also increased with the 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45 1000 1500 2000 2500 3000 Density (kg/m3) Compressive strength, f'c (MPa) Type A Type B Type C Type D Type E Type F Structural concrete Lightweight concrete Semi- lightweight Fig. 6. Classiﬁcation of concrete showing correlation between f 0 c and density. Table 8 Mer

improves the paste-aggregate transition zone in the long term. Such high quality paste-aggregate transition zone derived from interface characteristics and internal curing reduces the perme- ability of lightweight concrete compared to NC as observed in this study. Permeability also depends on W/B (increases with the increase of W/B as conﬁrmed from the permeability of series II VPC mix- tures – Table 7 and Fig. 10 ) and initial curing conditions [35,36]. For a constant W/B, the permeability decreased with the decrease of A/B (representing more porous lightweight aggregate) of all types of VPC mixes in Series I ( Fig. 11) as well as in series II ( Table 7). The 12-week water permeability of VPC mixes in Series II ran- ged between 3.4 /C2 10/C0 10 and 5.2 /C2 10/C0 10 cm/s ( Table 7 ). The replacement of Portland cement by PVPC also showed the beneﬁcial effect of reducing the 12-week water permeability of concrete. This was evident from the lower permeability of Types C and D mixes compared with Types A and B mixes, respectively in Series I ( Fig. 11). The pozzolanic reaction between ﬁnely ground pumice (present in PVPC) and calcium hydroxide produces a den- ser concrete as the age of concrete increases. The denser PVPC based VPC with the increase of age should exhibit lower perme- ability as conﬁrmed from this study. The reduction of permeability with age may have beneﬁcial effect of improving the long-term corrosion resistance of PVPC based VPCs similar to that observed in ﬂy ash concrete [37,38]. 3.5. Summary of properties and cost analysis of VPC mixtures Table 9 summarizes the range of mechanical and durability properties of developed VPC mixtures (in Series I and II) classiﬁed as lightweight-nonstructural, lightweight structural, semi-light- weight structural concretes. The study conﬁrms the potential of designing VPC incorporating pumice aggregate and PVPC with mechanical and durability characteristics conformable to other lightweight concrete using aggregates such as expanded slag, ex- panded clay, expanded slate, sintered ﬂy ash and scoria [2,10,25,29]. It should be noted that high drying shrinkage and comparatively low tensile strength may lead to the danger of shrinkage cracking in VPC mixes. However, the process of internal curing in VPCs can reduce the early age drying shrinkage. Lower E- values for VPCs indicate that there is a need for studies on struc- tural behaviours (to assess the viability of application of VPCs in actual construction) so that service stage parameters such as deﬂections and crack widths, are within allowable limits. The low- er permeability of VPCs will allow less moisture movement and may reduce the harmful effect of corrosion (in presence of chlo- rides) for protection of reinforcement. More investigations are needed on this aspect. Table 10 presents a cost analysis of the developed concrete mix- tures based on prices of Papua New Guinea regions where pumice is locally available; all ﬁgures are in Canadian dollars. The prices used were $115 a tonne for cement, $15 a tonne for coarse gravel aggregate, $8 a tonne for sand, $6 a tonne for coarse pumice aggre- gate, $10 a tonne for ﬁne pumice aggregate and $30 a tonne for ﬁ- nely ground pumice. For the places, where pumice is not found locally and is to be imported or transported, the cost of concrete will increase. Based on Table 10, the following information are de- rived: (i) about 10% reduction in cost (leading to a price of CAD$112 per m 3 compared

low-cost construction especially in volcanic areas. The following conclusions were drawn from the study: 1. The 28-day density, compressive/tensile strength and modulus of elasticity decreased (while air content increased) with the increase of pumice aggregate as replacement of normal coarse gravel aggregate/ﬁne aggregate (sand)/both. The use of PVPC with 20% pumice lowers the density, compressive/tensile strength and modulus of elasticity of VPC mixtures. Wide range of VPC mixtures satisfying the requirement of lightweight structural concrete (strength in excess of 15 MPa and density of less than 1850 kg/m 3) and semi-lightweight structural con- crete (strength in excess of 15 MPa and an air dry density rang- ing between 1850 kg/m 3 and 2150 kg/m 3) with varying aggregate-to-binder ratio ( A/B) and water-to-binder ratio ( W/ B) were proposed. 2. The 12-week drying shrinkage of VPC was higher compared with the representative NC (with 0% pumice aggregate) and the addi- tion of pumice aggregate as replacement of coarse gravel aggre- gate/sand/both increased the shrinkage. A maximum 34% increase in shrinkage was observed in VPCs with 100% coarse and ﬁne pumice aggregate compared with NC. The drying shrinkage also increased with the increase of W/B and with the decrease of A/B. VPC may be prone to shrinkage cracking due to its higher drying shrinkage and comparatively lower tensile strength compared to NC. On the other hand, danger of shrink- age cracking can be compensated by the lower modulus of elas- ticity. However, the use of PVPC in VPC mixtures has shown the beneﬁcial effect of reducing the drying shrinkage. 3. The 12-week water permeability of VPC mixtures with coarse/ ﬁne/both pumice aggregate were found to be less compared with NC (without pumice aggregate) and a maximum decrease of about 32% was observed. The lower permeability is attributed to the development of high quality paste-aggregate transition zone at the interface (conﬁrmed from the microhardness test- ing), reﬁnement of pore structure (conﬁrmed from the mercury intrusion porosimetry) and the progressive internal curing in VPCs. PVPC based VPCs also exhibited lower 12-week perme- ability due to the pozzolanic reaction of ﬁnely ground pumice (as conformed from the differential scanning calorimetry) that produced a denser concrete. Such beneﬁcial effect of pumice either as aggregates or pozzolans can improve the long-term corrosion resistance of VPCs. 4. Further investigations on important parameters such as corro- sion of steel and carbonation are needed to assess the suitability of application of VPCs in actual structures. Cement Association of Canada; 2002. [4] Singh M

Lightweight concrete incorporating pumice based blended cement and aggregate: Mechanical and durability characteristics K.M.A. Hossain a,⇑, S. Ahmed b, M. Lachemi a a Department of Civil Engineering, Ryerson University, Toronto, Ontario, Canada b Sustainable Construction Limited, Chittagong, Bangladesh article info Article history: Received 15 November 2009 Received in revised form 4 August 2010 Accepted 2 September 2010 Available online 25 September 2010 Keywords: Volcanic pumice Blended cement Lightweight concrete Mix design Strength Durability Interfacial transition zone Internal curing abstract This paper presents the development of lightweight volcanic pumice concrete (VPC) using pumice based ASTM Type I blended cement (PVPC) and aggregates (both coarse and ﬁne). The performance of VPC mix- tures was evaluated by conducting comprehensive series of tests on fresh and hardened properties as well as durability. Fresh and mechanical properties of VPC mixtures such as slump, air content, compres- sive strength, tensile strength, density, and modulus of elasticity are described. The durability character- istics were investigated by drying shrinkage, water permeability, mercury intrusion porosimetry, differential scanning calorimetry and microhardness tests. The variables in the study include: % replace- ment (0%, 50%, 75% and 100% by volume) of normal weight coarse gravel aggregate by coarse lightweight volcanic pumice aggregate (VPA), replacement (100% by volume) of ﬁne aggregate (sand) by ﬁne VPA, constant (0.45)/variable (0.37–0.64) water-to-binder ratio by mass, variable (1.3–3.7) aggregate-to-bin- der ratio by mass and cement types (ASTM type I cement and PVPC). The investigation suggests the pro- duction of lightweight VPCs for structural applications having satisfactory strength and durability characteristics. The use of PVPC induces the beneﬁcial effect of reducing the drying shrinkage and water permeability of VPC mixtures. The presence of coarse/ﬁne/both VPA is also associated with lower perme- ability due to the development of high quality interfacial paste-aggregate transition zone and the pro- gressive internal curing in VPCs. Development of non-expensive and environmentally friendly VPC with acceptable strength and durability characteristics (as illustrated in this study) can be extremely helpful for the sustainable construction and rehabilitation of volcanic disaster areas around the world. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Research has been conducted worldwide on a large number of natural (such as diatomite, pumice, scoria, sawdust, oil palm shells, and bottom ash) or artiﬁcial (such as expanded shale, slag, slate, perlite, and vermiculite) aggregates to manufacture mortar and concrete [1–10]. Use of natural lightweight aggregates instead of processed artiﬁcial aggregates can signiﬁcantly reduce cost of such concretes. Finding new and improved ways to build with natural volcanic materials (such as pumice and ash) is becoming wide- spread and their use as construction materials can lead to low-cost construction [1,8,9]. The use of these volcanic materials as replace- ment of Portland cement in blended cement production can also lead to lower greenhouse gas emission and sustainable construc- tion [1,8,9]. Globally, 19.6 Mt of volcanic pumice (VP) is mined every year with Turkey remaining the dominant producer with a production of 4.2 Mt per year [11]. Other leading countries are Italy, Chile, Ecua

compared with normal concrete (Mix A-0-PC-S) with the same workability (Tables 4 and 5 ). To make a VPC with 50–100% pumice aggregate (as replacement of gravel aggregate) having satisfactory workability, the range of slump values should be between 47 mm and 75 mm (as illustrated from the current study of Series I: Table 4). The slump value decreased with the increase of lightweight coarse pumice aggregate in mixes (A–D) of Series I ( Table 4 ). The slump values of concrete mixtures in Series I and II also decreased due to the replacement of Portland cement by PVPC (Type C < Type A; Type B < Type D; Type F < Type E) ( Tables 4 and 5 ). For VPC mixes in series II, slump value ranged between 53 mm and 70 mm ( Table 5 ). The slump values also decreased signiﬁcantly due to the replacement of sand by lightweight ﬁne pumice aggre- gate (slump values of Types B, D, F are less compared to Types A, B and E, respectively) ( Table 5 ). The air content of Series I concrete mixtures increased with the increase of coarse pumice aggregate (for all mix types) and with the replacement of Portland cement by PVPC (Type C > Type A and Type D > Type B) ( Table 4). The air content also increased with the replacement of sand by ﬁne pumice aggregate in Series I (Type B > Type A and Type D > Type C) and Series II (Type F > Type E) con- crete mixtures ( Tables 4 and 5 ). In series II, the air content of VPC mixtures ranged between 2.7% and 4.2%. Lowering of slump and increase of air content in concrete mix- tures due to the use of coarse/ﬁne pumice aggregate and PVPC can be attributed to the low density and high porosity of pumice. De- spite lower slump values (compared with NC), all VPC mixtures were found to be cohesive and workable as well as showed no segregation. 3.2. Mechanical properties of lightweight pumice concrete The mechanical properties such as density, compressive/tensile strength and modulus of elasticity of concrete mixtures in Series I and II are summarized in Tables 6 and 7 , respectively. 3.2.1. Density The 28-day density decreased with the increase of coarse pum- ice aggregates as replacement of gravel aggregates and the replace- ment Portland cement by PVPC in Series I ( Table 6 ). The replacement of sand by ﬁne pumice aggregate also lowered the density of both Series I and II concrete mixtures ( Tables 6 and 7 ). This was due to the replacement of comparatively heavier Portland cement/gravel aggregate by lighter ﬁnely ground pumice/pumice aggregate. A maximum decrease of about 42% (from 2514 to 1463 kg/m3) was observed in Type D-100%VPA (Mix: D-100-PVPC) concrete compared to NC with 0% VPA (Mix: A-0-PC-S). 3.2.2. Compressive/tensile strength and modulus of elasticity The 28-day compressive/tensile strength and modulus of elas- ticity decreased with the increase of % coarse pumice aggregate from 0% to 100% (for all mix types in Series I) ( Table 6 ). The strength/modulus of elasticity also decreased with the replace- ment of sand by ﬁne pumice aggregate in Series I (Type B > Type A and Type D > Type C: Figs. 1 and 2 ) and Series II (Type F > Type E; Table 7) concrete mixtures. This can be attributed to the replace- ment of strong gravel aggregate by relatively weak pumice aggre- gate and the decrease of mortar content. The strength/modulus of elasticity of PVPC concrete mixtures are also found to be lower compared with Portland cement based ones (Type C > Type A and Type D > Type B) ( Fig. 1 ). nely divided silica in pumice can combine with calcium hydroxide liberated by the hydrating Portland cement in the presence of water to form stable cementitious compounds) can contribute to the enhancement of long-term strength and durability [8,20–22]. Such beneﬁcial effect on strength is not observed within 28-day as the reduction of strength due to cement replacement by ﬁnely ground pumice was not compensated [12]. A maximum decrease of about 60% for f 0 c (from 40 to 16 MPa) and ft (from 3.7 to 1.5 MPa) was observed in Mix: D-100-PVPC compared to NC (Fig. 3 ). A maximum decrease of about 60% for f 0 c (from 40 to 16 MPa) and ft (from 3.7 to 1.5 MPa) was observed in Mix: D- 100-PVPC compared to NC ( Fig. 3 ). A maximum decrease of about 67% for E (from 3.7 to 1.5 GPa) was observed in Mix: D-100-PVPC compared to NC ( Fig. 3 ). Elastic modulus of lightweight aggregate concrete usually ranges between 40% and 80% of normal concrete of the same strength [25]. The 28-day elastic modulus of Series II VPC mixes ranged between 5.8 and 11.4 GPa ( Table 7 ). All Series I VPCs (Types B and D) with a coarse pumice aggregate replacement within the range of 50% and 100% of gravel aggregate by volume developed compressive strength ranging between 16 MPa and 22 MPa ( >15 MPa) and an air dry density ranging between 1462 kg/m3 and 1820 kg/m 3 (<1850 kg/m3) to satisfy the criteria for lightweight structural concrete ( Table 6 ) [26]. Other VPC mix- tures in Series I with strength >15 MPa and density >1850 kg/m 3 can be used as semi-lightweight structural concrete ( Table 6). In Series II, the 28-day compressive strength of VPC mixes ranges between 10 and 27 MPa ( Table 7 ). VPC mixtures in Series II with compressive strength >15 MPa and density less than 1850 kg/m3 can be used as lightweight structural concrete ( Table 7). However, lower strength VPC (<15 MPa) can be used in the manufacture of lightweight building blocks and other building products. 3.2.3. Correlation between mechanical properties The modulus of elasticity of concrete ( E) is a function of density (d) and compressive strength ( f 0 c ). The formulas presented in ACI 318 [27], deﬁnes this relationship in terms of either square root of f 0 c or combination of density ( d) and square root of f 0 c as follows: E ¼ 4:73 ﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ f 0 c q ð1Þ E ¼ 43 /C2 10/C0 6d 1:5 ﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ f 0 c q ð2Þ where E is in GPa, d is in kg/m 3 and f 0 c is in MPa. Eq. (2) is applicable for values of d ranging between 1440 and 2480 kg/m 3 and for strength levels of 21–35 MPa. Fig. 2 shows that the modulus of elasticity ( E) of the VPC mix- tures (developed in this study) can be expressed as a function of density (d) and compressive strength ( f 0 c ). The following equations are developed based on the experimental data ( Fig. 4 ): E ¼ 2:0046 ﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ f 0 c q ð3Þ E ¼ 30 /C2 10/C0 6d 1:5 ﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ f 0 c q ð4Þ However, Eqs. (3) and (4) should be valid for the range of VPC mix- tures developed in this study. Correlation between f 0 c and ft also exists for the VPC mixtures developed in this study ( Fig. 5 ). Developed concrete mixtures are also classiﬁed based on f 0 c and density in Fig. 6. Correlation also ex- ists between compressive strength and density ( Fig. 6 ). As ex- pected, compressive strength of VPCs increases with the increase of density for each type of concrete mixes. 3.3. Micro-structural properties 3.3.1. Porosity and pore size distribution The porosity and average pore diameter as measured by MIP are reported in Tabl

2.1.2. Series II concrete mixtures Series II mixes were developed with variable W/B (0.37–0.64 by mass), binder content (280–480 kg/m 3) and A/B (1.3–3.7 by mass). Series II mixes were classiﬁed into two types. Type E VPC mixtures were developed by using PVPC, coarse pumice aggregate and sand while Type F mixtures incorporated PVPC, coarse and ﬁne pum- ice aggregates. Mix design of these eight VPC mixtures are presented in Table 5 . 2.2. Testing details All 24 concrete mixtures were prepared in a laboratory counter-current mixer. Pumice aggregates were made saturated surface dry before mixing by immersing in water for 24 h and then trowelling the surface. The mixing sequence consisted of 3 min of mixing the aggregates with water followed by additional 3 min of mixing with cement. Comprehensive series of tests on fresh, mechanical and durability properties of concrete mixtures were conducted. The slump of fresh concrete was determined as per ASTM C 143. Air contents for NC (0VPA) and lightweight concrete were determined as per ASTM C 231 and ASTM C173/173 M, respectively. 28-day compressive ( f 0 c ) and indirect tensile ( ft), strengths were determined by using 100 /C2 200-mm cylinders as per ASTM C 39 and ASTM C 496, respectively. 100 /C2 200 mm cylinders were also used for modulus of elasticity ( E) and density. The drying shrinkage ( DS)o f7 5 /C2 75 /C2 285-mm spec- imens were monitored according to ASTM C 157 every week for a total of 12 weeks. The 12-week water permeability ( a) was determined (after 1 day of moist and remaining days of air curing of 100 /C2 200 mm cylinder specimens) by applying 1.4 MPa of water pressure in a hydraulic permeability apparatus. The samples from 100 /C2 200 mm cylinder specimens (1 day of moist and remaining days of air curing for a total period of 12 weeks) were used for the mer- cury intrusion porosimetry (MIP), differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and microhardness tests. The porosity and pore size distribution were measured using the MIP technique with an apparatus having a measuring pressure ranging from 0.01 to 200 MPa [23]. The DSC test was used to determine the quantity of Ca(OH) 2 formed in the mortar. Based on the ASTM E 384-99 testing method, a Vickers inden- ter was used to determine the microhardness in the transition zone between aggre- gates and paste using slices cut from the middle of the concrete cylinders. As the typical width of the interfacial transition zone (ITZ) is about 50 lm, the applied load in the microhardness test was determined to be 0.05 N so that the spacing between indentation points should be at least two times the diagonal of the indentation while ensuring a sufﬁcient number of points was taken to map the ITZ [24] Six determinations were performed on the surface of each sample, and the proﬁles of microhardness yielded by the average values were derived. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Fresh properties of lightweight pumice concrete The fresh properties of concrete mixtures are presented in Ta- bles 4 and 5 .
